text: The surgeon begins mobilization of the sigmoid colon, carefully freeing it up to the level of the descending colon. This step is crucial for ensuring a tension-free colorectal anastomosis and aids in freeing the ureter during endometriosis surgery.

The surgeon releases flimsy adhesions of the bowel near the previous hysterectomy site, revealing a deep endometriotic nodule at the vault. The ureter is identified and mobilized on the left side at the bifurcation of the common iliac vessels, followed by bladder mobilization for clear visibility of the vault. A probe is visible inside the vault. The same steps are repeated on the right side, with the peritoneum opened, ureter lateralized, and the inferior hypogastric nerve identified, ensuring preservation of the hypogastric plexus.

The surgeon identifies the inferior hypogastric nerve and opens the space lateral and medial to it, leading into the presacral space. Using gentle traction, counter traction, and blunt dissection, the presacral space is opened to free the rectum from the posterior aspect. Careful dissection is performed to avoid damage to nerves and venous plexuses, ensuring complete mobilization of the rectum and preservation of bowel function. Similar dissection steps are carried out on the opposite side to preserve the nerve bundle.

The surgeon begins dissection at the sacral promontory to identify the inferior hypogastric nerve, using gentle traction and counter traction to open the perisacral space with laparoscopic shearer. The rectum is mobilized completely, and dissection proceeds medial to the utero sacral ligament into the presacral space. Both sides of the presacral space are merged, allowing identification and mobilization of the inferior hypogastric nerve and superior hypogastric plexus. Dissection continues to the liver trini muscle, ensuring complete lateralization of nerves. The surgeon then addresses a 2.5 by 1.9 cm nodule causing luminal narrowing over the rectum, identified by MRI.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic shearer to dissect around the nodule, entering the Danone-Wheeler's fascia above it. The fascia is gently freed, exposing the anterior and lateral parts of the rectum. Dissection continues to the rectoanal junction, identified by perpendicular vessels. The bowel is mobilized, and fat tissue is cleared to prepare for transverse resection and colorectal anastomosis. The sigmoid mesentery is mobilized to the aortic bifurcation, allowing the sigmoid and proximal rectum to be positioned in the pelvis. An endoscopic stapler is used to fire transversely below the rectal nodule, with one or two rounds of stapling needed.

The surgeon mobilizes the bowel in all planes to ensure it can be securely grasped by the stapler for complete transection. A mini laparotomy incision is made to extract the proximal stump, and an anvil is inserted after resecting approximately five centimeters of the rectum. The circular stapler is passed, and the anvil is fitted inside, ensuring the bowel is correctly positioned before firing. The stapler is closed and fired, creating two loops of bowel for a tension-free anastomosis. A drain is placed, and peritoneal lavage is performed at the end of the surgery.
